▌ClearBank Receives MiCA Approval, Plans to Launch Stablecoin and Deepen Partnership with Coinbase

On April 13, Netherlands-based ClearBank received approval from the Dutch Financial Markets Authority (AFM) to operate as a Crypto Asset Service Provider (CASP) under the Crypto Asset Markets Regulation Act (MiCA). ClearBank plans to offer EURC and USDC stablecoin services to support more efficient cross-border transfers and payments. Furthermore, ClearBank will deepen its partnership with Coinbase, including offering FSCS-covered savings accounts through Coinbase. ClearBank Europe is currently authorized by the European Central Bank and regulated by the Dutch Central Bank (DNB).

▌Michael Saylor Hints at Imminent Bitcoin Purchases

On April 13, “Looking to the future,” Michael Saylor said on Sunday while sharing a chart of Strategy’s Bitcoin purchase history, which has become a sign of impending Bitcoin accumulation. According to the company, Strategy’s most recent Bitcoin purchase was on April 6, when it bought 4,871 Bitcoins, worth over $329.8 million, bringing its total holdings to 766,970 Bitcoins, worth approximately $54.5 billion at the market price at the time of publication.

...

▌Due to geopolitical conflicts and energy issues, several mining farms are selling Bitcoin

On April 13, data showed that MARA Holdings sold more than 15,000 Bitcoins in March to reduce leverage, Riot Platforms sold more than 3,700 Bitcoins in the first quarter, Cango sold 2,000 Bitcoins to repay Bitcoin-backed debt, and Bitdeer stated that as of February 20, its Bitcoin holdings had dropped to zero.

▌Justin Sun accuses WLFI of having a hidden blacklist backdoor in its token contract

On April 13, Justin Sun accused WLFI of embedding a secret blacklist function in its WLFI smart contract, giving it the power to unilaterally freeze token holders' wallets without prior notice or any remedy.

He stated that his wallet was frozen in 2025. Justin Sun wrote on X: "This is a trapdoor disguised as an open door." Important Economic Developments International crude oil prices surged 9.4% on Monday, with WTI crude rising 9.4% to $104.54 per barrel, following the failure of the weekend talks between the US and Iran and Trump's blockade of Iranian maritime traffic. All members of the US negotiating team have left Pakistan. A US official stated on April 12 that all members of the US negotiating team had left Pakistan after the US-Iran talks concluded. The official stated that personnel including Witkov, Kushner, and the technical team did not remain in the Pakistani capital, indicating that the two sides would not immediately resume direct negotiations. Earlier that day, Vance said at a press conference in Islamabad, the Pakistani capital, that the US had not yet reached a consensus with Iran and would return to the United States. The US-Iran negotiations in Islamabad ended on the 12th without any agreement. The Iranian side said the negotiations were conducted in an "atmosphere of distrust and suspicion," with disagreements on two or three key issues. The US side stated that it had clearly stated its "red lines," but Iran did not accept the US conditions. (CCTV News) (Xinhua News Agency) Trump Threatens to Strike Iranian Economy by Controlling Strait of Hormuz On April 12, US President Trump retweeted an article on his social media, stating that if Iran refuses to compromise, its trump card might be controlling the Strait of Hormuz through the US Navy to prohibit Iranian oil tankers from entering or leaving, thereby striking at the Iranian economy. (CCTV News) According to CME's "FedWatch" on April 10, the probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 1.6%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 98.4%. The probability of the Federal Reserve cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 2.1%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 96.3%, and the probability of cumulatively raising interest rates by 25 basis points is 1.5%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Stocks surge after "ceasefire," but Wall Street warns: damage has already been done.
data.description:
The news of a ceasefire in the Middle East briefly boosted risk assets, but Wall Street strategists warned that the war has left deep wounds on inflation, energy supplies, and the Federal Reserve's policy space that are difficult to heal quickly. This week, benchmark U.S. stock indexes rose across the board, with the S&P 500 surging 3.6%, its biggest weekly gain since the end of November last year. However, the S&P's rally weakened in midday trading on Friday, closing lower as markets worried whether the peace talks this weekend could end the six-week-long war that has deeply impacted the economy.

Institutions maintain target prices while simultaneously lowering them

Currently, most institutions have chosen to maintain their full-year target prices for the time being, but their reasons and confidence vary.

Luca Paolini, chief strategist at Pictet Asset Management, said that influenced by Tuesday's ceasefire news, the team, which was close to adjusting its positions, has postponed its actions. They now expect the S&P 500 to close at 7250 points by the end of the year, European stocks to rise by about 10%, and the 10-year US Treasury yield to fall below 4.25%.

Pictet expects the Federal Reserve and the Bank of England to each cut interest rates once, while the European Central Bank will remain on hold.

Scott Chronert, head of US equity strategy at Citigroup, maintained his optimistic forecast released in mid-December, arguing that "most of the current situation appears to be transitional."

However, he also acknowledged that risks cannot be ignored, including persistently high oil prices leading to prolonged high interest rates, pressure on the private credit market, and potential disruptions from AI and Trump's tariff policies. He pointed out that upward revisions to earnings expectations are concentrated in a few large-cap stocks such as Nvidia and Broadcom, indicating that market rotation has been significantly hampered. He said: "This is a market still searching for direction, and it is too early to make definitive judgments." Wells Fargo was one of the few institutions to lower its full-year forecast, reducing its S&P 500 target price from 7800 to 7300. Chief equity strategist Ohsung Kwon believes the current economy is less sensitive to oil prices than in past cycles, and tax refunds will partially offset pressure on household consumption. However, Kwon says: "Unless we see a significant deterioration in earnings-driven growth, we still expect the stock market to perform solidly throughout the year."
